title: The Epistemology of Disgust: When Moral Intuitions Mislead
summary: A philosophical essay examining how the emotion of disgust has historically masqueraded as moral reasoning, the psychological mechanisms that underlie this confusion, and why sophisticated ethical thinking requires vigilance against the seductive authority of visceral reactions.
body:
Few emotions wear the costume of moral authority so convincingly as disgust. When confronted with acts they find repugnant, people routinely report not merely that those acts displease them, but that those acts are wrong — that the feeling of revulsion is itself evidence of an ethical verdict. What the philosopher Jonathan Haidt termed the 'moral dumbfounding' effect reveals just how precarious this assumption is: subjects, when presented with harmless yet taboo scenarios, typically condemn the acts as immoral but are unable to articulate any coherent reason for their condemnation beyond restating their disgust. Asked why the act is wrong, they say, in effect, that it simply is — that the feeling says so. This is not moral reasoning; it is moral emotion masquerading as argument.

The deeper problem is not that disgust is always a poor guide, but that it is systematically miscalibrated to features of the modern moral landscape. Disgust evolved as a pathogen-avoidance system. Its original targets were contaminated food, bodily waste, and disease vectors — precisely the stimuli for which a finely tuned aversion reflex would have enhanced survival. That the system has been co-opted to regulate social behaviour, particularly around sexuality, purity, and group membership, is a contingent fact of cultural evolution rather than a reflection of any intrinsic moral signal. The philosopher Martha Nussbaum has argued at length that the extension of disgust from biological to social targets is not merely unjustified but actively dangerous, insofar as it licenses the dehumanisation of stigmatised groups by associating them with contamination and animal functions.

Nor should we conclude from this critique that moral intuitions as a class are epistemically worthless. The question is one of calibration and context. Strong intuitions that converge across cultures and that survive reflective scrutiny — the wrongness of gratuitous cruelty, for instance — carry genuine evidential weight, however difficult it may be to provide a systematic justification for them. What distinguishes these intuitions from disgust-driven condemnations is that the former tend to survive the process that philosophers call 'reflective equilibrium': the iterative adjustment of intuitions and principles until they form a coherent whole. Disgust-based verdicts, by contrast, tend to crumble under sustained examination, precisely because their genealogy is not moral but biological.

The practical stakes are considerable. Legal systems that have incorporated the 'reasonable man's revulsion' as a partial defence in cases of violence against marginalised groups have effectively encoded visceral prejudice into the architecture of justice. Arguments against same-sex relationships, against the consumption of non-normative but harmless food sources, against various forms of consensual intimacy — all have leaned heavily on the rhetoric of the 'unnatural' and the 'disgusting', without those terms doing any independent moral work. Stripped of the emotional charge, the arguments reduce to: I find this upsetting, therefore it should be prohibited. No serious ethical theory, whether deontological, consequentialist, or virtue-based, can validate that inference.

What would it mean, then, to discipline moral intuition without discarding it? The answer likely involves a combination of historical literacy — understanding how disgust has been weaponised — and the cultivation of what might be called 'epistemic humility about the gut'. This is not the elimination of emotion from ethics; emotions, as the philosopher Ronald de Sousa has argued, are themselves a form of perception, attuned to features of situations that purely propositional reasoning may miss. It is, rather, the refusal to allow any single emotional signal — however powerful, however widely shared — to function as a conversation-stopper. The moment we treat revulsion as self-validating, we have abandoned the project of ethics in favour of the mere rationalisation of instinct.

annotations.meaningVi: sự ngơ ngác về đạo đức — phán xét mà không đưa ra được lý do
annotations.exampleEn: Moral dumbfounding occurs when people condemn an act but cannot explain why beyond restating their discomfort.

annotations.meaningVi: ghê tởm, đáng phẫn nộ về mặt đạo đức
annotations.exampleEn: The committee found the proposal morally repugnant and refused to put it to a vote.

annotations.meaningVi: sự ghê tởm sâu sắc, cảm giác kinh hãi
annotations.exampleEn: Her revulsion at the description was immediate and visceral.

annotations.meaningVi: hệ thống né tránh mầm bệnh — cơ chế tiến hóa để tránh nhiễm bệnh
annotations.exampleEn: Disgust is best understood as a pathogen-avoidance system that evolved before language or culture.

annotations.meaningVi: bị tiếp quản/chuyển đổi mục đích — sử dụng một cơ chế vào mục đích khác ban đầu
annotations.exampleEn: A neural mechanism originally evolved for one purpose can be co-opted to serve an entirely different function.

annotations.meaningVi: sự thật ngẫu nhiên — không nhất thiết phải như vậy
annotations.exampleEn: That English uses subject-verb-object order is a contingent fact, not a logical necessity.

annotations.meaningVi: sự phi nhân hoá — tước bỏ phẩm giá con người
annotations.exampleEn: Propaganda frequently employs dehumanisation to make violence against enemies seem acceptable.

annotations.meaningVi: vô giá trị về mặt nhận thức luận — không mang thông tin có thể tin cậy
annotations.exampleEn: A belief formed through wishful thinking is not necessarily false, but it is epistemically worthless.

annotations.meaningVi: cân bằng phản tư — điều chỉnh qua lại giữa trực giác và nguyên tắc đến khi nhất quán
annotations.exampleEn: Rawls used reflective equilibrium as a method to build an ethical theory from considered moral judgements.

annotations.meaningVi: điều chỉnh lặp đi lặp lại — cải tiến qua nhiều vòng
annotations.exampleEn: Good design emerges from iterative adjustment — testing, revising, and testing again.

annotations.meaningVi: phả hệ / nguồn gốc — lịch sử hình thành của một ý niệm hoặc thực hành
annotations.exampleEn: Nietzsche's genealogy of morals traced ethical concepts back to their historical and social origins.

annotations.meaningVi: định kiến bản năng — thành kiến xuất phát từ cảm xúc ruột thịt chứ không phải lý trí
annotations.exampleEn: Laws built on visceral prejudice tend to collapse when subjected to impartial judicial scrutiny.

annotations.meaningVi: cấu trúc của hệ thống pháp luật — cách tổ chức nền tảng của nền tư pháp
annotations.exampleEn: Reformers argued that bias was not incidental but had been built into the very architecture of justice.

annotations.meaningVi: luận điệu về sự phản tự nhiên — dùng từ 'tự nhiên'/'phi tự nhiên' như lập luận đạo đức
annotations.exampleEn: The rhetoric of the unnatural has been used across history to oppose everything from left-handedness to interracial marriage.

annotations.meaningVi: thuộc về đạo đức bổn phận — đánh giá hành động dựa trên quy tắc/nghĩa vụ
annotations.exampleEn: A deontological approach holds that some acts are wrong regardless of their consequences.

annotations.meaningVi: thuộc về thuyết hậu quả — đánh giá đạo đức dựa trên kết quả của hành động
annotations.exampleEn: A consequentialist would justify deception if it produced significantly better outcomes for all involved.

annotations.meaningVi: điều ngăn chặn cuộc thảo luận — lập luận kết thúc tranh luận không phải bằng lý lẽ
annotations.exampleEn: Appeals to national security are often used as a conversation-stopper rather than a genuine argument.

annotations.meaningVi: sự hợp lý hoá bản năng — dùng lý lẽ để che đậy phản ứng bản năng
annotations.exampleEn: What looks like a principled argument can sometimes be no more than the rationalisation of instinct.

annotations.meaningVi: sự tàn nhẫn vô cớ — gây đau khổ mà không có lý do chính đáng nào
annotations.exampleEn: Even moral relativists tend to agree that gratuitous cruelty — harm that serves no purpose — is wrong.

annotations.meaningVi: sự khiêm tốn nhận thức — thừa nhận giới hạn của những gì mình biết
annotations.exampleEn: Epistemic humility requires acknowledging that your most confident beliefs might turn out to be mistaken.

annotations.meaningVi: lý luận mệnh đề — suy luận dựa trên các phát biểu có thể đúng hoặc sai
annotations.exampleEn: Propositional reasoning alone cannot capture the felt moral significance of a specific human situation.

annotations.meaningVi: giả dạng như, ngụy trang thành
annotations.exampleEn: Self-interest masquerading as principle is one of the most common forms of motivated reasoning.

grammarNotes.title: Inversion After Negative and Restrictive Adverbs
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Trong tiếng Anh học thuật, khi một trạng từ mang nghĩa phủ định hoặc hạn chế được đưa lên đầu câu, trật tự chủ-động từ bị đảo ngược (inversion). Trong bài: 'Nor should we conclude from this critique that moral intuitions as a class are epistemically worthless.' Các từ kích hoạt đảo ngữ gồm: Nor, Neither, Never, Not only, Rarely, Seldom, Hardly, Scarcely, Little. Cấu trúc: Nor/Never/Rarely + trợ động từ + chủ ngữ + động từ chính. Đây là cấu trúc đặc trưng của văn viết học thuật và cho thấy trình độ C1-C2.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Nor should we conclude...' is a subject-auxiliary inversion triggered by the negative adverb 'nor' at the start of a clause. Inversion is required after: Nor, Neither, Never, Not only, Rarely, Seldom, Hardly, Scarcely, Little. Pattern: Nor/Never/Rarely + auxiliary + subject + main verb. In informal speech, inversion is often dropped, but in formal and academic writing it is obligatory and marks a sophisticated register. Contrast: 'We should not conclude either...' (neutral) vs. 'Nor should we conclude...' (formal, emphatic).

grammarNotes.title: Nominalisations and Abstract Noun Phrases
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Tiếng Anh học thuật thường biến động từ và tính từ thành danh từ trừu tượng (nominalisation). Ví dụ trong bài: 'the rationalisation of instinct' (← to rationalise), 'the dehumanisation of stigmatised groups' (← to dehumanise), 'the cultivation of epistemic humility' (← to cultivate). Cấu trúc danh từ hoá thường đi kèm 'of' + danh từ bổ sung. Ưu điểm: giúp diễn đạt ý tưởng phức tạp cô đọng, chuyên nghiệp hơn. Nhược điểm: nếu lạm dụng sẽ làm câu nặng nề, khó hiểu.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: Nominalisations — converting verbs or adjectives into abstract nouns — are a hallmark of academic prose. In this essay: 'the rationalisation of instinct' (rationalise → rationalisation), 'the dehumanisation of stigmatised groups' (dehumanise → dehumanisation), 'the calibration and context' (calibrate → calibration). These noun phrases pack more information into fewer words and allow complex ideas to serve as grammatical subjects. Use nominalisations to build academic register, but balance them with active verb constructions to preserve clarity.

translationVi:
Ít cảm xúc nào mặc bộ trang phục của quyền uy đạo đức thuyết phục như sự ghê tởm. Khi đối mặt với các hành động mà họ thấy đáng phẫn nộ, mọi người thường không chỉ nói rằng những hành động đó làm họ khó chịu, mà còn khẳng định rằng chúng là sai trái — rằng cảm giác kinh hãi bản thân nó đã là bằng chứng cho một phán quyết đạo đức. Hiệu ứng 'ngơ ngác đạo đức' mà nhà triết học Jonathan Haidt đặt tên cho thấy giả định này bấp bênh như thế nào: các đối tượng, khi được trình bày các tình huống vô hại nhưng cấm kị, thường lên án hành động là vô đạo đức nhưng không thể đưa ra bất kỳ lý do mạch lạc nào ngoài việc lặp lại sự ghê tởm của họ. Khi được hỏi tại sao hành động đó sai, họ nói rằng nó đơn giản là sai — rằng cảm xúc nói như vậy. Đây không phải là lập luận đạo đức; đây là cảm xúc đạo đức giả dạng thành lập luận.

Vấn đề sâu xa hơn không phải là sự ghê tởm luôn là kim chỉ nam tồi, mà là nó bị hiệu chỉnh sai một cách có hệ thống với các đặc điểm của bối cảnh đạo đức hiện đại. Sự ghê tởm đã tiến hóa như một hệ thống né tránh mầm bệnh. Các mục tiêu ban đầu của nó là thực phẩm bị ô nhiễm, chất thải cơ thể, và các vật mang mầm bệnh. Việc hệ thống này bị chiếm dụng để điều chỉnh hành vi xã hội, đặc biệt xung quanh tình dục, sự thuần khiết, và tư cách thành viên nhóm, là một sự thật ngẫu nhiên của tiến hóa văn hóa hơn là phản ánh của bất kỳ tín hiệu đạo đức nội tại nào. Triết gia Martha Nussbaum đã lập luận rằng sự mở rộng của sự ghê tởm từ mục tiêu sinh học sang mục tiêu xã hội không chỉ vô căn cứ mà còn nguy hiểm.

Cũng không nên kết luận từ phê bình này rằng các trực giác đạo đức nói chung là vô giá trị về mặt nhận thức. Câu hỏi là về hiệu chỉnh và ngữ cảnh. Các trực giác mạnh mẽ hội tụ qua các nền văn hóa và tồn tại được qua sự xem xét kỹ lưỡng mang trọng lượng bằng chứng thực sự. Điều phân biệt chúng với các phán xét dựa trên sự ghê tởm là chúng có xu hướng tồn tại qua quá trình 'cân bằng phản tư'. Các phán quyết dựa trên sự ghê tởm, ngược lại, có xu hướng sụp đổ dưới sự kiểm tra kéo dài.

Phải hiểu thế nào về việc kỷ luật trực giác đạo đức mà không loại bỏ nó? Câu trả lời có thể liên quan đến sự kết hợp giữa học vấn lịch sử và việc trau dồi điều có thể gọi là 'khiêm tốn nhận thức về ruột gan'. Đây không phải là loại bỏ cảm xúc khỏi đạo đức; cảm xúc, như triết gia Ronald de Sousa đã lập luận, bản thân chúng là một dạng tri giác. Mà đây là sự từ chối cho phép bất kỳ tín hiệu cảm xúc đơn lẻ nào — dù mạnh mẽ, dù được chia sẻ rộng rãi — hoạt động như một chốt chặn cuộc trò chuyện.
criticalThinkingQuestion: The essay argues that disgust-based moral verdicts 'tend to crumble under sustained examination, precisely because their genealogy is not moral but biological.' Does the evolutionary origin of a moral intuition automatically disqualify it as evidence? Could one argue that a disgust response that is both universal and adaptive is tracking something morally significant, even if imperfectly?

questions.prompt: What does Jonathan Haidt's 'moral dumbfounding' effect demonstrate, according to the essay?
options: That people are unable to form moral judgements without consulting external authorities
That subjects condemn harmless taboo acts as immoral but cannot give coherent reasons beyond restating their disgust
That disgust is a reliable indicator of genuine ethical wrongdoing in most cultures
That moral intuitions always conflict with philosophical reasoning
questions.explanation: The essay states: 'subjects, when presented with harmless yet taboo scenarios, typically condemn the acts as immoral but are unable to articulate any coherent reason for their condemnation beyond restating their disgust.'
questions.hint: What happens when these subjects are asked to explain why they condemn the act?

questions.prompt: According to the essay, what was the original evolutionary purpose of disgust?
options: To regulate social hierarchies and group membership
To enforce cultural norms around sexuality and purity
To avoid contaminated food, bodily waste, and disease vectors
To signal moral violations to other members of a social group
questions.explanation: 'Disgust evolved as a pathogen-avoidance system. Its original targets were contaminated food, bodily waste, and disease vectors.'
questions.hint: What kind of 'system' does the essay say disgust originally evolved as?

questions.prompt: Why does Martha Nussbaum consider the extension of disgust to social targets 'actively dangerous'?
options: Because it overwhelms rational deliberation and makes policy-making impossible
Because it licenses the dehumanisation of stigmatised groups by associating them with contamination
Because it leads individuals to adopt overly conservative dietary habits
Because it makes societies more susceptible to disease by misdirecting hygiene instincts
questions.explanation: 'The philosopher Martha Nussbaum has argued at length that the extension of disgust from biological to social targets is... actively dangerous, insofar as it licenses the dehumanisation of stigmatised groups by associating them with contamination and animal functions.'
questions.hint: What harmful process does Nussbaum say this extension of disgust 'licenses'?

questions.prompt: What criterion does the essay suggest distinguishes epistemically reliable moral intuitions from disgust-driven condemnations?
options: Reliable intuitions are always endorsed by at least one major religious tradition
Reliable intuitions are those that can be expressed in formal logical notation
Reliable intuitions survive reflective equilibrium — the iterative adjustment of intuitions and principles
Reliable intuitions are those shared by more than half of all human cultures studied
questions.explanation: 'What distinguishes these intuitions from disgust-driven condemnations is that the former tend to survive the process that philosophers call reflective equilibrium: the iterative adjustment of intuitions and principles until they form a coherent whole.'
questions.hint: What philosophical process do reliable intuitions survive but disgust-based verdicts do not?

questions.prompt: What does the essay say happens to disgust-based moral verdicts under sustained examination?
options: They become more entrenched and harder to challenge
They tend to crumble because their genealogy is biological, not moral
They gain broader cultural acceptance over time
They are reformulated as philosophical principles
questions.explanation: 'Disgust-based verdicts, by contrast, tend to crumble under sustained examination, precisely because their genealogy is not moral but biological.'
questions.hint: What does the essay say their 'genealogy' is?

questions.prompt: What practical consequence does the essay identify from legal systems incorporating 'reasonable man's revulsion'?
options: Courts have become more impartial in applying criminal penalties
Visceral prejudice has been effectively encoded into the architecture of justice
Defence lawyers have gained unfair advantages in violent crime cases
Public support for the judiciary has declined significantly
questions.explanation: 'Legal systems that have incorporated the reasonable man's revulsion as a partial defence... have effectively encoded visceral prejudice into the architecture of justice.'
questions.hint: What has been 'encoded' into the legal system as a result?

questions.prompt: How does the essay characterise arguments against same-sex relationships that rely on the language of 'unnatural' and 'disgusting'?
options: As culturally specific but internally coherent ethical positions
As virtue-based arguments that deserve more philosophical attention
As arguments that, stripped of emotional charge, reduce to: I find this upsetting, therefore it should be prohibited
As consequentialist positions that fail only because they miscalculate outcomes
questions.explanation: 'Stripped of the emotional charge, the arguments reduce to: I find this upsetting, therefore it should be prohibited. No serious ethical theory... can validate that inference.'
questions.hint: What does the essay say these arguments 'reduce to' once their emotional charge is removed?

questions.prompt: According to the essay, what is Ronald de Sousa's view of emotions?
options: Emotions are unreliable distortions that philosophy must overcome
Emotions are themselves a form of perception, attuned to features of situations that propositional reasoning may miss
Emotions are evolutionary adaptations that have no role in modern ethical life
Emotions should be expressed openly but never used as the basis for moral judgements
questions.explanation: 'emotions, as the philosopher Ronald de Sousa has argued, are themselves a form of perception, attuned to features of situations that purely propositional reasoning may miss.'
questions.hint: De Sousa describes emotions as a form of what?

questions.prompt: What does the essay identify as the key risk when revulsion is treated as 'self-validating'?
options: We risk suppressing legitimate scientific research into human behaviour
We risk abandoning the project of ethics in favour of the mere rationalisation of instinct
We risk making moral philosophy inaccessible to ordinary people
We risk undermining democratic deliberation by privileging emotion over data
questions.explanation: 'The moment we treat revulsion as self-validating, we have abandoned the project of ethics in favour of the mere rationalisation of instinct.'
questions.hint: What project does the essay say we abandon when we treat revulsion as self-validating?

questions.prompt: What does the essay mean by calling the co-optation of disgust 'a contingent fact of cultural evolution'?
options: It is a deliberate choice made by societies to enforce moral norms
It happened by chance and necessity, not because it reflects any intrinsic moral signal
It is a consequence of genetic mutation rather than social learning
It was inevitable given the biological structure of the human brain
questions.explanation: 'That the system has been co-opted to regulate social behaviour... is a contingent fact of cultural evolution rather than a reflection of any intrinsic moral signal.'
questions.hint: What does 'contingent' imply about whether this had to happen?

questions.prompt: What does the essay argue that 'disciplining moral intuition' requires?
options: Replacing all emotional responses with formal logical analysis
Historical literacy and epistemic humility about the gut
Adopting a single consistent ethical framework such as consequentialism
Conducting cross-cultural surveys to verify which intuitions are universal
questions.explanation: 'The answer likely involves a combination of historical literacy — understanding how disgust has been weaponised — and the cultivation of what might be called epistemic humility about the gut.'
questions.hint: What two things does the essay say the answer 'likely involves'?

questions.prompt: Which three ethical theories does the essay mention cannot validate the inference 'I find this upsetting, therefore it should be prohibited'?
options: Pragmatism, relativism, and natural law theory
questions.explanation: 'No serious ethical theory, whether deontological, consequentialist, or virtue-based, can validate that inference.'
questions.hint: The essay lists three categories of ethical theory when dismissing this inference.

questions.prompt: What is the writer's overall stance on the role of emotion in ethics?
options: Emotions should be entirely excluded from ethical reasoning
Emotions are more reliable guides than philosophical principles in most real-world situations
Emotions have a role as perception but must not function as conversation-stoppers
Only emotions that are universally shared across cultures should be admitted as evidence
questions.explanation: The essay states emotions 'are themselves a form of perception' but warns against allowing any single emotional signal 'to function as a conversation-stopper.'
questions.hint: What metaphor does the essay use for what emotions should not become in ethical discourse?

questions.prompt: What example of a moral intuition does the essay cite as carrying 'genuine evidential weight' because it survives reflective scrutiny?
options: The wrongness of consuming taboo foods
The wrongness of violating cultural purity norms
questions.explanation: 'Strong intuitions that converge across cultures and that survive reflective scrutiny — the wrongness of gratuitous cruelty, for instance — carry genuine evidential weight.'
questions.hint: What specific example does the essay give of an intuition that passes the test of cross-cultural convergence?

questions.prompt: Which inference best captures the essay's argument about the relationship between an emotion's origin and its moral authority?
options: Emotions with biological origins are always more trustworthy than culturally acquired ones
An emotion's biological origin does not disqualify it entirely, but it does mean its moral authority must be scrutinised rather than assumed
Only emotions that can be given a systematic philosophical justification have any moral validity
The more widely shared an emotional response, the stronger its claim to moral authority
questions.explanation: The essay does not dismiss all intuitions as biologically derived, but argues that disgust-based verdicts must be subjected to 'sustained examination' precisely because their origin is biological rather than moral.
questions.hint: Does the essay say biology automatically disqualifies a response, or does it argue for scrutiny rather than dismissal?

cloze.template:
Few emotions wear the costume of moral authority so convincingly as disgust. When confronted with acts they find repugnant, people routinely report not merely that those acts displease them, but that those acts are wrong — that the feeling of {{b1}} is itself evidence of an ethical verdict. What the philosopher Jonathan Haidt termed the 'moral {{b2}}' effect reveals just how precarious this assumption is: subjects, when presented with harmless yet taboo scenarios, typically condemn the acts as immoral but are unable to articulate any coherent reason for their condemnation beyond restating their disgust. Asked why the act is wrong, they say, in effect, that it simply is — that the feeling says so. This is not moral reasoning; it is moral emotion {{b3}} as argument.

The deeper problem is not that disgust is always a poor guide, but that it is systematically {{b4}} to features of the modern moral landscape. Disgust evolved as a pathogen-avoidance system. Its original targets were contaminated food, bodily waste, and disease vectors — precisely the stimuli for which a finely tuned aversion reflex would have enhanced survival. That the system has been {{b5}} to regulate social behaviour, particularly around sexuality, purity, and group membership, is a contingent fact of cultural evolution rather than a reflection of any intrinsic moral signal. The philosopher Martha Nussbaum has argued at length that the extension of disgust from biological to social targets is not merely unjustified but actively dangerous, insofar as it licenses the {{b6}} of stigmatised groups by associating them with contamination and animal functions.

Nor should we conclude from this critique that moral intuitions as a class are epistemically worthless. The question is one of calibration and context. Strong intuitions that converge across cultures and that survive reflective scrutiny — the wrongness of gratuitous cruelty, for instance — carry genuine evidential weight, however difficult it may be to provide a systematic justification for them. What distinguishes these intuitions from disgust-driven condemnations is that the former tend to survive the process that philosophers call 'reflective {{b7}}': the {{b8}} adjustment of intuitions and principles until they form a coherent whole. Disgust-based verdicts, by contrast, tend to crumble under sustained examination, precisely because their {{b9}} is not moral but biological.

The practical stakes are considerable. Legal systems that have incorporated the 'reasonable man's revulsion' as a partial defence in cases of violence against marginalised groups have effectively encoded {{b10}} prejudice into the architecture of justice. Arguments against same-sex relationships, against the consumption of non-normative but harmless food sources, against various forms of consensual intimacy — all have leaned heavily on the rhetoric of the 'unnatural' and the 'disgusting', without those terms doing any independent moral work. Stripped of the emotional charge, the arguments reduce to: I find this upsetting, therefore it should be prohibited. No serious ethical theory, whether {{b11}}, consequentialist, or virtue-based, can validate that inference.

What would it mean, then, to discipline moral intuition without discarding it? The answer likely involves a combination of historical literacy — understanding how disgust has been weaponised — and the cultivation of what might be called 'epistemic {{b12}} about the gut'. This is not the elimination of emotion from ethics; emotions, as the philosopher Ronald de Sousa has argued, are themselves a form of {{b13}}, attuned to features of situations that purely propositional reasoning may miss. It is, rather, the refusal to allow any single emotional signal — however powerful, however widely shared — to function as a {{b14}}. The moment we treat revulsion as {{b15}}, we have abandoned the project of ethics in favour of the mere rationalisation of instinct.

blanks.explanation: The essay says the feeling of revulsion is mistakenly treated as evidence of an ethical verdict.
Haidt's term for the effect where people condemn acts but cannot explain their condemnation.
The essay says moral emotion is 'masquerading as argument' — wearing its costume.
Disgust is 'systematically miscalibrated' to the modern moral landscape.
The system has been co-opted — repurposed from its original biological function.
Nussbaum says disgust extended to social targets licenses the dehumanisation of stigmatised groups.
The philosophical process called 'reflective equilibrium' involves adjusting intuitions and principles iteratively.
Reflective equilibrium involves the 'iterative adjustment' — repeated cycles of revision.
The essay says disgust-based verdicts crumble because their 'genealogy is not moral but biological'.
Prejudice encoded in legal systems is described as 'visceral' — gut-based rather than rational.
The essay lists 'deontological, consequentialist, or virtue-based' as the three categories of serious ethical theory.
The essay calls for 'epistemic humility about the gut' — not trusting visceral reactions uncritically.
De Sousa argues emotions are a form of perception, attuned to situational features.
The essay warns against allowing emotional signals to function as 'conversation-stoppers'.
Treating revulsion as 'self-validating' means accepting it as its own justification — the essay's central warning.
